data.title: Peru central bank holds benchmark interest rate at 4.25%
data.description:
Peru's central bank on Thursday held its benchmark interest rate at 4.25% for the third straight meeting, in line with analysts' expectations.

The decision comes after Peru's statistics agency reported inflation up 0.11% in November, bringing the 12-month benchmark rate for the capital Lima and wider metropolitan area to 1.37% -towards the lower edge of the bank's 1% to 3% target range.

"Year-on-year inflation is projected to gradually approach the center of the target range in the coming months. Likewise, inflation excluding food and energy is estimated to remain around 2%," the central bank said in a statement.

Peru's central bank has gradually brought its rate down from a high of 7.75% it maintained through the first part of 2023.

The central bank added that an easing of global trade tensions would likely foster moderate economic growth.

The Andean nation remains one of South America's most economically stable countries despite regular political upheaval, including Congress' October ouster of President Dina Boluarte.

General elections are scheduled for April 2026.

data.title: Lululemon Expects New Strategy to Show Up in Inventory This Spring — Market Talk
data.description: Lululemon's current assortment doesn't reflect its current strategy, but that should change this spring, Finance Chief Meghan Frank says during a call with analysts. "The assortments you currently see in stores and online include certain styles that are not representative of our go forward vision for the brand," she says, noting the company has let product life cycles run too long in some of its key franchises. Lululemon has been working to bring a new energy into its assortment, and increase its speed and agility, she says. The company remains on track for new styles to make up 35% of its assortment in the spring, she says. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: NZX 50 Extends Gains but Points to Weekly Losses
data.description:
The NZX 50 rose 44 points, or 0.3%, to 13,440 in early deals on Friday, extending gains from the previous session, mainly supported by communication services, healthcare, consumer discretionary, and utilities.

Interest rate cuts by the Fed continued to lift sentiment, though it signaled fewer reductions for 2026.

Traders anticipated the release of a slew of economic data next week, including GDP figures for Q3, amid hopes of a better reading after the RBNZ cut interest rates.

On the data front, business PMI improved to the highest level in four months in November, driven by new orders, production, and employment, indicating an improvement in domestic demand.

Among early gainers were Fisher & Paykel (1.3%), Fonterra (1.1%), Mercury NZ (1.1%), Freightways (0.7%), Meridian Energy (0.9%), Infratil (0.7%), Ebos Group (0.6%), and ANZ Group (0.3%).

For the week, the index is heading for a 0.3% decline, after being flat in the previous week.

data.title: Cycle Group to Acquire Applied Therapeutics
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Cycle Group Holdings Limited will acquire by the pharmaceutical company Applied Therapeutics.

Cycle will buy Applied for an initial price of 8.8 cents a share, Applied said Thursday. The company had around 145.6 million shares outstanding as of Nov. 13, according to a filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission, implying an initial transaction value of roughly $12.8 million.

As part of the agreement Cycle will issue to shareholders non-transferrable contingent value rights entitling them to two, 10 cent payments per share. That money will be paid upon approval of Applied's treatments for galactosemia and Charcot-Marie-Tooth sorbitol dehydrogenase deficiency within eight years of closing. Investors will also get a 20 cent payment per share if sales from Applied's products exceed $200 million within 10 years of closing.

Applied is also issuing an unsecured promissory note to Cycle allowing it to take loans up to $8.5 million to use as working capital.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.description:

data.title: PLATTS: 755--Platts Weekly US Renewable Energy Certificate (REC) Commentary - 11Dec25
data.description: (Platts)--11Dec2025/608 pm EST/2308 GMT **California REC prices remain steady across buckets **Texas REC prices mostly decline for various vintages **PJM contracts show mixed movements over the week US renewable energy certificates exhibited stable prices in California, while prices in Texas generally decreased. Meanwhile, PJM contracts showed mixed movements during the week ended Dec. 11. On Dec. 11, a utility told Platts: "Current market inactivity [in California] is likely due to participants having met their compliance reporting needs, suggesting increased movement may resume in mid to late January 2026". California REC prices across various buckets remained stable, with no changes recorded in the closing values. The California REC Bucket 3 for the 2025 vintage closed at $3.95, while the 2026 vintage also held steady at $5.45. Furthermore, in California, for the Bundled REC categories, the 2025 vintage of Bucket 1 closed at $16.15, with the 2026 vintage maintaining a price of $19.20. Similarly, Bucket 2, the 2025 vintage, was assessed at $8.15, and the 2026 vintage remained at $12.95. Texas REC prices reflected a downward trend, with the non-solar 2026 vintage closing at $2.32, a decrease of $0.04/MWh, while the SREC 2026 vintage mirrored this decline, also closing at $2.32. The Green-e Eligible Wind REC 2025 vintage (BH) fell by $0.10/MWh, closing at $2.05, while the 2025 FH vintage remained unchanged at $1.95. In Maryland, SREC prices showed a decreasing trend, with the 2025 vintage closing at $53.15, a decrease of $0.35/MWh from the previous week. The 2026 vintage also fell by $0.35/MWh, closing at $43.65. In New Jersey, REC Class 1 contracts experienced mixed movements; the 2025 vintage decreased by $0.05/MWh, closing at $25.7, and the 2026 vintage dropped $0.13/MWh to $25.97. However, the 2027 vintage experienced an increase of $0.12/MWh, ending at $26.07. Pennsylvania AEC Tier 1 prices for both the 2025 and 2026 vintages showed declines, closing at $23.47 and $23.62, respectively, each down $0.08/MWh. However, the 2027 vintage saw a gain of $0.30/MWh, closing at $23.20. The AEC Tier 2 2026 vintage rose significantly by $1.80/MWh, closing at $28.50. Within the DC market, the REC Tier 1 prices fell, with the 2024 vintage down $0.90/MWh to $23.00 and the 2025 vintage down $0.10/MWh to $23.65. Platts is part of S&P Global Energy. — Jose Del angel, jose.del.angel@spglobal.com-- Edited by Giselle Rodriguez, newsdesk@spglobal.com--Platts Electricity Alert--

data.title: The SEC's Lone Democrat Fears Darkness Awaits Investors — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Bill Alpert

Caroline Crenshaw's last speech before ending her term as a member of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission was dark. Even doomy.

"I am the lone Democrat at the SEC," she told an audience at the Brookings Institution think tank on Thursday. After Jan. 3, the commission may find itself with three Republican members and no dissenting voice.

"I fear that the very core of our intricate market structure is under attack," she said. "And instead of safeguarding our markets for investors to fund their retirements in safe and sustainable ways, we are moving in a direction where markets start to look like casinos."

She added: "The problem with casinos, of course, is that in the long run, the house always wins."

The White House didn't immediately respond to a request for comment on Crenshaw's speech.

Since President Donald Trump's election put Republicans in charge of the securities regulator, Crenshaw said her colleagues have worked to reduce disclosure, shareholder proxy access, and antifraud enforcement. At the same time, the Republican-led agency has lent its ear to hidden industry voices and preoccupied itself with promoting cryptocurrency initiatives that personally benefit the Trump family, she said.

Crenshaw said her Republican colleagues say their program will revitalize public markets and "make IPOs great again." They need not do so by punishing investors and the agency's public servants, she said.

The SEC has been far from perfect, Crenshaw allowed. Yet its bipartisan history has found consensus and curbed policy swings across administrations, she said. If Trump leaves the commission with all Republicans — as he has at other federal agencies — the resulting extremes may be reversed by a later administration, she said.

"Markets like predictability," Crenshaw said. "What markets don't like is rules changing every four years."

Among the other looming changes that dismay her are the addition of private equity assets to workers' 401(k) retirement plans and trading alterations that could make publicly displayed stock prices more scarce.

Crenshaw said the agency has brought fewer enforcement cases and dismissed existing cases "left and right," often in parallel with the president's pardoning of criminals who committed "massive white-collar frauds."

Experienced staff have been lost with headcount reductions of 15% to 20%. "When we remove the tools that detect fraud and make it less costly to commit fraud, people will commit more fraud. It's as simple as that," Crenshaw said.

The departing commissioner tried to brighten her dark canvas with glimpses of a future when the agency returns to work on behalf of everyday Americans. But her mood seemed pretty gloomy.

"We're being told that 'Today is a new day at the commission'," Crenshaw said. "But with each successive day, the night grows longer. I fear that the darkest depths of winter still lie ahead for America's capital markets."

Write to Bill Alpert at william.alpert@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

Patrick L. Alexander, a 10% owner and director of Landmark Bancorp, sold 9,000 shares of common stock on December 11, 2025.
